title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Megan Clark, who created a language learning app.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're talking to Megan Clark, who created a language learning app. Megan, you created an app for learning languages. What gave you the idea? || Guest: I was learning Italian before a holiday, and I downloaded several apps. They were useful, but I noticed I could answer exercises without really speaking. [20]The idea came when I wanted short conversations I could practise on the bus[/20]. I did not want long grammar lessons; I wanted something simple that made me say useful sentences out loud, even if I only had five minutes. || Interviewer: What was difficult when you were starting out? || Guest: At first, I tried to build too many features. I planned games, videos, tests and a travel guide, and the app became confusing. [21]A friend who tested it told me to make one part work really well before adding more[/21]. That was difficult to hear, but she was right. I removed half the buttons and focused on short speaking practice. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy most about it now? || Guest: The biggest surprise was who used the app. I expected young travellers, and many did, but [22]the most regular users were adults learning because of their jobs[/22]. They did not always need perfect grammar. They needed to welcome a customer, answer the phone or explain a simple problem. So we added more work situations, such as hotels, shops and offices. || Interviewer: Have you changed the way you work since then? || Guest: Recording the voices was important. A computer voice can be clear, but learners also need natural rhythm. [23]We asked real teachers to record slowly, but not like robots[/23]. They repeated key phrases, left pauses and sounded friendly. Users told us that made them less embarrassed when they practised alone at home or on the way to work. || Interviewer: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work? || Guest: I read user comments every week, although not every idea can be used. The comment that changed the app most was about progress. [24]People wanted to see what they could do, not just how many lessons they had finished[/24]. Now the app says things like, You can order food or You can ask for directions. That feels more useful than a simple score. || Interviewer: And what are your plans for the future? || Guest: Next year I want to make the app better for groups. Many schools use it, but teachers need easier ways to follow students. [25]We are developing a classroom page where teachers can choose practice tasks for the whole class[/25]. Students would still speak on their own phones, but the teacher could see which topics need more work. || Interviewer: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar? || Guest: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like language learning app, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal. || Interviewer: Thank you, that was really interesting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're talking to Megan Clark, who created a language learning app. Megan, you created an app for learning languages. What gave you the idea?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I was learning Italian before a holiday, and I downloaded several apps. They were useful, but I noticed I could answer exercises without really speaking. The idea came when I wanted short conversations I could practise on the bus. I did not want long grammar lessons; I wanted something simple that made me say useful sentences out loud, even if I only had five minutes.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was difficult when you were starting out?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: At first, I tried to build too many features. I planned games, videos, tests and a travel guide, and the app became confusing. A friend who tested it told me to make one part work really well before adding more. That was difficult to hear, but she was right. I removed half the buttons and focused on short speaking practice.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy most about it now?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The biggest surprise was who used the app. I expected young travellers, and many did, but the most regular users were adults learning because of their jobs. They did not always need perfect grammar. They needed to welcome a customer, answer the phone or explain a simple problem. So we added more work situations, such as hotels, shops and offices.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Have you changed the way you work since then?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Recording the voices was important. A computer voice can be clear, but learners also need natural rhythm. We asked real teachers to record slowly, but not like robots. They repeated key phrases, left pauses and sounded friendly. Users told us that made them less embarrassed when they practised alone at home or on the way to work.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I read user comments every week, although not every idea can be used. The comment that changed the app most was about progress. People wanted to see what they could do, not just how many lessons they had finished. Now the app says things like, You can order food or You can ask for directions. That feels more useful than a simple score.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: And what are your plans for the future?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Next year I want to make the app better for groups. Many schools use it, but teachers need easier ways to follow students. We are developing a classroom page where teachers can choose practice tasks for the whole class. Students would still speak on their own phones, but the teacher could see which topics need more work.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like language learning app, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Thank you, that was really interesting.

questions.question: Why did Megan first think of the app?

options.A: She wanted to practise short conversations anywhere.
options.B: She needed grammar lessons for a holiday.
options.C: She wanted to make travel videos.

questions.explanation: Megan says the idea came when she wanted short conversations she could practise on the bus.

questions.question: What advice did Megan's friend give her?

options.A: Add more games to the app.
options.B: Improve one feature before adding others.

questions.explanation: Megan's friend told her to make one part work really well before adding more.

questions.question: Who used the app most regularly?
questions.explanation: Megan says the most regular users were adults learning because of their jobs.

questions.question: What was important about the recorded voices?

options.A: They were all recorded by actors.

questions.explanation: Megan says real teachers recorded slowly, but not like robots, and sounded friendly.

questions.question: What did users want the app to show?

options.A: what practical things they could do
options.B: how many mistakes they had made

questions.explanation: Users wanted to see what they could do, not just how many lessons they had finished.

questions.question: What is Megan developing for schools?

options.A: a set of printed grammar books

questions.explanation: Megan is developing a classroom page where teachers can choose practice tasks for the whole class.
